title: Which property makes containers run identically in dev, staging, and prod?
content:
Quiz — Container Benefits

Q: Which container property ensures the same image runs in dev, staging, and production environments?

Choices: A. Portable  B. Scalable  C. Continuously deployable  D. Declarative

Answer: C — The continuous deployment property guarantees the container image is identical across all environments, eliminating environment drift.

title: What does 'declarative format' mean for containers?
content:
Quiz — Container Benefits

Q: What does it mean that containers use a declarative format?

Choices: A. You write step-by-step instructions for how to build the container  B. You describe the desired result and let the system figure out how to achieve it  C. You declare which programming language the app uses  D. You specify the exact order of container startup

Answer: B — Declarative means you describe WHAT you want (e.g., 3 replicas of nginx), not HOW to get there. Kubernetes reconciles the actual state to match.

title: Why do containers need orchestration at scale?
content:
Quiz — Container Benefits

Q: At what point do containers require orchestration tools like Kubernetes?

Choices: A. When running more than 1 container  B. Only when using Docker Compose  C. When managing hundreds of hosts with thousands of containers  D. Orchestration is always optional

Answer: C — 1-2 containers on one host is trivial. Once you scale to hundreds of hosts with thousands of containers, manual scheduling, networking, and health management is impossible without orchestration.

title: What is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?
content:
Quiz — Pods and Services

Q: What is the basic unit of scaling and resiliency in Kubernetes?

Choices: A. Container  B. Pod  C. Deployment  D. Node

Answer: B — A Pod is the smallest deployable unit. It can contain one or more containers that share the same network namespace and optional volumes.

title: What does a Kubernetes Service provide?
content:
Quiz — Pods and Services

Q: What is a Kubernetes Service?

Choices: A. A stable endpoint (IP + DNS name) that routes traffic to matching pods  B. A running container inside a pod  C. A configuration file for the cluster  D. A network namespace shared between nodes

Answer: A — A Service gives pods a stable ClusterIP and DNS name. kube-proxy uses iptables rules to route to healthy pods matching the Service's label selector.

title: How do containers within the same Pod communicate?
content:
Quiz — Pods and Services

Q: How do containers within the same Pod communicate with each other?

Choices: A. Via the Service ClusterIP  B. Via the node's IP address  C. Via localhost because they share a network namespace  D. Via a dedicated inter-container network bridge

Answer: C — Containers in the same Pod share a network namespace. They communicate via localhost:port. Each container gets a different port but the same IP.

title: Which control plane component stores all cluster state?
content:
Quiz — Kubernetes Internals

Q: Which control plane component is the authoritative store of all Kubernetes cluster state?

Choices: A. kube-apiserver  B. kube-scheduler  C. etcd  D. kube-controller-manager

Answer: C — etcd is the distributed key-value store that holds ALL cluster state. Every kubectl get/apply ultimately reads from or writes to etcd. Losing etcd means losing the cluster state.

title: What is the role of the kube-controller-manager?
content:
Quiz — Kubernetes Internals

Q: What does the kube-controller-manager do?

Choices: A. Routes traffic between pods  B. Runs the reconciliation control loops (desired state vs actual state)  C. Assigns pods to nodes  D. Stores cluster configuration

Answer: B — The controller manager runs controllers like the ReplicaSet controller, which continuously checks if the actual number of pods matches the desired count and creates/deletes pods to reconcile.

title: What are predicates in Kubernetes scheduling?
content:
Quiz — Pod Scheduling

Q: What are Kubernetes scheduler predicates?

Choices: A. Scores that determine the optimal node for pod placement  B. Rules that determine if a pod CAN be scheduled on a node  C. Annotations that control pod affinity  D. Resource limits applied after pod creation

Answer: B — Predicates are hard filters (pass/fail). A node must pass ALL predicates for a pod to be scheduled there. Examples: resource fit, taints, affinity, volume availability.

title: What happens when you set resource requests on a pod?
content:
Quiz — Pod Scheduling

Q: What does setting resources.requests.cpu on a pod do?

Choices: A. Sets a hard limit on CPU usage  B. Tells the scheduler the guaranteed CPU needed — used to find eligible nodes  C. Overrides the node's CPU allocation  D. Enables CPU throttling for the container

Answer: B — Requests tell the scheduler the GUARANTEED resource needed. The scheduler finds nodes with enough remaining allocatable resources. Limits are the maximum; requests drive scheduling.

title: What does kubectl apply -f do?
content:
Quiz — kubectl and Objects

Q: What happens when you run kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml?

Choices: A. Deletes the resources defined in the file  B. Creates or updates resources declaratively — if exists, patches to match file  C. Always creates new resources even if they already exist  D. Only works for Pod objects

Answer: B — apply is declarative. If the resource doesn't exist, it creates it. If it does exist, it patches it to match the manifest. Idempotent. Unlike kubectl create which fails if resource exists.

title: What is the purpose of a DaemonSet?
content:
Quiz — kubectl and Objects

Q: What is a Kubernetes DaemonSet used for?

Choices: A. Running a fixed number of pod replicas  B. Running exactly one pod on every node for node-level agents  C. Scheduling periodic background jobs  D. Managing stateful applications with persistent storage

Answer: B — A DaemonSet ensures one pod runs on every node. Used for: Fluent Bit (log collection), node-exporter (metrics), aws-node (VPC CNI). NOTE: DaemonSets are NOT supported on AWS Fargate.

title: How does a Deployment enable zero-downtime updates?
content:
Quiz — Workloads

Q: How does a Kubernetes Deployment perform a rolling update?

Choices: A. It deletes all old pods first, then creates new ones  B. It creates a new ReplicaSet and gradually shifts pods from old to new  C. It clones all pods simultaneously then deletes the originals  D. It stops all traffic to the service during the update

Answer: B — A Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet for the new version. It scales up the new RS while scaling down the old RS one pod at a time. kubectl rollout undo reverses this.

title: When should you use a StatefulSet instead of a Deployment?
content:
Quiz — Workloads

Q: In which scenario would you use a StatefulSet instead of a Deployment?

Choices: A. Stateless web frontend with many replicas  B. MySQL database where each replica needs its own stable storage and identity  C. Batch job that runs once and exits  D. Log collector running on every node

Answer: B — StatefulSets are for stateful apps needing stable pod names (mysql-0, mysql-1), stable storage per pod, and ordered startup/shutdown. Use Deployments for stateless apps where pods are interchangeable.

title: Where are Kubernetes Secrets stored and how are they encoded?
content:
Quiz — Workloads

Q: What is the default storage mechanism for Kubernetes Secrets?

Choices: A. Encrypted with AES-256 in etcd  B. Stored as plain text on disk  C. Base64 encoded and stored in etcd (NOT encrypted by default)  D. Encrypted with AWS KMS automatically

Answer: C — Secrets are base64 encoded (NOT encrypted) in etcd by default. base64 is reversible encoding, not encryption. Enable envelope encryption with KMS for real security.

title: Reel: Container Benefits
content:
Reel script — Container Benefits

HOOK: Stop repackaging your app for every environment.

SCRIPT:
Containers solved a 20-year deployment nightmare.
One image runs the same everywhere — dev, staging, prod.
No more 'works on my machine' excuses.
They're portable: any OS that runs containers runs your app.
They're scalable: add more copies in seconds, remove them just as fast.
They're declarative: describe what you want, not how to build it.
Think of it like a shipping container.
The same box goes on a truck, a train, or a ship — unchanged.
That's your Docker image.
Five properties: portable, cloud deployable, scalable, continuously deployable, declarative.
Master these and you'll understand every design decision in Kubernetes.
That's containers in 60 seconds.

VISUAL_CUE: Shipping container crossing between truck, train, ship — then split screen showing same container on EC2, ECS, EKS
HASHTAGS: #kubernetes #containers #docker #aws #devops

title: Reel: Pods and Services
content:
Reel script — Pods and Services

HOOK: A pod is not a container. This distinction will save you on the exam.

SCRIPT:
A Pod is the smallest unit Kubernetes knows about.
It wraps one or more containers in a shared network namespace.
Containers inside a Pod talk to each other via localhost.
Every Pod gets its own IP address.
But pods are temporary — they restart with new IPs.
That's the problem. Your app can't hardcode a pod IP.
Enter: the Service.
A Service gives you a stable ClusterIP that never changes.
kube-proxy sets iptables rules to route traffic to healthy pods.
Label selectors are the glue — pods with app:backend belong to the backend Service.
Three things to memorise: Pod equals network unit, Service equals stable endpoint, labels equal the connection.

VISUAL_CUE: Pod with two containers sharing localhost, then pod dying and respawning with new IP, then Service abstracting the change
HASHTAGS: #kubernetes #pods #services #k8s #cloudnative

title: Reel: Kubernetes Internals
content:
Reel script — Kubernetes Internals

HOOK: Four components run Kubernetes. Know them or fail the exam.

SCRIPT:
The Kubernetes control plane has four critical components.
kube-apiserver: every kubectl command goes through here.
etcd: the cluster's brain. Stores ALL state. Never lose etcd.
kube-scheduler: decides which node gets each pod.
kube-controller-manager: watches for drift and fixes it.
Here's the magic: Kubernetes uses a control loop.
Desired state lives in etcd. Actual state is what's running.
Controllers compare the two. If they differ, they act.
This reconciliation loop is why Kubernetes is self-healing.
Worker nodes run kubelet, kube-proxy, and your containers.
On AWS EKS, the control plane lives in an AWS-managed account.
You never touch those four components. AWS runs them for you.

VISUAL_CUE: Control plane box with four labeled components, worker nodes on right, reconciliation loop animation
HASHTAGS: #kubernetes #controlplane #eks #aws #k8sexplained

title: Reel: Pod Scheduling
content:
Reel script — Pod Scheduling

HOOK: The scheduler is choosing your pod's home right now. Here's how.

SCRIPT:
When you create a pod, it starts as Pending.
The scheduler has one job: find the best node.
First it filters: which nodes CAN run this pod?
Resource fit, taints, node affinity, volume requirements.
Nodes that fail any filter are eliminated immediately.
Then it scores the survivors.
Spread the pods? Pack them tight? Prefer a specific zone?
The highest-scoring node wins.
Resource requests are the key input — not limits.
Set requests too low: pod lands on a busy node, gets throttled.
Set them too high: pod can't schedule, stays Pending forever.
Right-sizing requests is one of the most impactful K8s optimisations.

VISUAL_CUE: Pending pod → filter 5 nodes → eliminate 3 → score 2 → place on winner
HASHTAGS: #kubernetes #scheduling #k8s #devops #awseks

title: Reel: kubectl essentials
content:
Reel script — kubectl essentials

HOOK: Five kubectl commands. Learn these first.

SCRIPT:
kubectl is your window into the cluster.
kubectl get pods — see what's running right now.
kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml — create or update resources declaratively.
kubectl describe pod nginx-abc — full details plus events. Debugging starts here.
kubectl logs my-pod -f — stream live logs from a container.
kubectl exec -it my-pod -- bash — get a shell inside a running container.
The secret: apply is declarative and idempotent.
Run it a hundred times, same result.
Unlike kubectl create, which fails if the resource exists.
Always use apply in scripts and CI/CD.
Keep your manifests in Git. Apply them from Git. That's GitOps in one line.

VISUAL_CUE: Terminal with each command running, output appearing, split to show effect in cluster diagram
HASHTAGS: #kubectl #kubernetes #k8s #devops #cloudnative

title: Reel: K8s Workload types
content:
Reel script — K8s Workload types

HOOK: Wrong workload type = midnight incident. Pick the right one.

SCRIPT:
Kubernetes has four main workload types. Each for a different job.
Deployment: stateless apps. Pods are identical and interchangeable. Rolling updates built in.
StatefulSet: stateful apps like databases. Pods get stable names: mysql-0, mysql-1. Each gets its own storage.
DaemonSet: run one pod on every node. Log collectors, monitoring agents, CNI plugins. Not on Fargate.
Job and CronJob: run to completion. Batch jobs, cron tasks. Not always-on.
The exam loves this question: when do you use StatefulSet vs Deployment?
Stateful means data must survive pod restarts and each instance has unique identity.
Examples: MySQL, Kafka, Redis, Elasticsearch.
Everything else is a Deployment.

VISUAL_CUE: Four workload types as four lanes, examples flowing through each lane
HASHTAGS: #kubernetes #deployments #statefulset #daemonset #k8s

title: Step 1 — Container properties visual
content:
Lab step 1 — Container Benefits: Container properties visual

Narration: Containers have five key properties that make them ideal for modern cloud applications: portable, cloud deployable, scalable, continuously deployable, and declarative.

Visual: Five boxes in a row, each labelled with one property. Each box animates in with a brief description and an analogy (shipping container for portable, clouds for cloud deployable, scaling arrows for scalable).

Key fact: Declarative means you describe WHAT you want, not HOW to build it — Kubernetes reconciles actual to desired state.

title: Step 2 — Monolith vs microservices
content:
Lab step 2 — Container Benefits: Monolith vs microservices

Narration: A monolith bundles all services into one deployable. Microservices split each function into an independent service, each with its own containers.

Visual: Left side shows one large box with all services (UI, logic, orders, DB) tightly coupled. Right side explodes this into small independent boxes connected by arrows, each in its own container.

Key fact: Containers are the perfect unit for microservices because they are independently deployable and scalable.

title: Step 1 — Pod anatomy
content:
Lab step 1 — Pods and Services: Pod anatomy

Narration: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It wraps one or more containers that share the same network namespace — they can communicate via localhost.

Visual: Outer dashed-border box labelled Pod with IP 10.0.1.5. Inside: two smaller boxes (Container A: nginx:8080, Container B: log-agent) with a shared eth0 icon. An arrow shows localhost:8080 communication.

Key fact: All containers in a Pod share one IP address and communicate via localhost. A Pod lives and dies as a unit.

title: Step 2 — Services as stable endpoints
content:
Lab step 2 — Pods and Services: Services as stable endpoints

Narration: Pods get new IPs when they restart. A Service solves this with a stable ClusterIP and DNS name. kube-proxy routes traffic to any healthy Pod with the matching label.

Visual: Show a pod dying (red flash) and coming back with a different IP. Then show a Service box with ClusterIP 10.100.0.26 pointing to three pods regardless of their changing IPs. Label selector app:backend highlighted.

Key fact: Never hard-code pod IPs. Always use Service DNS names. Services decouple consumers from pod lifecycle.

title: Step 1 — Control plane components
content:
Lab step 1 — Kubernetes Internals: Control plane components

Narration: The Kubernetes control plane has four components: kube-apiserver (the front door), etcd (cluster state store), kube-scheduler (pod placement), and kube-controller-manager (reconciliation).

Visual: Two-zone diagram. Left zone labelled Control Plane with four stacked boxes: API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager. Right zone labelled Data Plane with Worker Node boxes containing kubelet, kube-proxy, and pod icons.

Key fact: On EKS, AWS manages the entire control plane. You never SSH into those four components.

title: Step 2 — Reconciliation control loop
content:
Lab step 2 — Kubernetes Internals: Reconciliation control loop

Narration: Kubernetes uses a control loop: observe actual state, compare with desired state, act to reconcile any difference. This loop never stops.

Visual: Circular flow diagram. Box 1: Desired State (YAML: replicas: 3). Box 2: Observe (kubectl get pods: 2 running). Box 3: Diff (need 1 more). Box 4: Act (create pod). Arrow returns to Observe. Green check if equal: no action needed.

Key fact: This self-healing loop is why Kubernetes recovers from node failures automatically — the desired state always wins.

title: Step 1 — Predicates filter nodes
content:
Lab step 1 — Pod Scheduling: Predicates filter nodes

Narration: The scheduler first applies predicates — hard rules that eliminate nodes that cannot host the pod. A node failing any predicate is immediately excluded.

Visual: Pending pod on the left. Arrow to a list of 5 nodes. Nodes with red X filtered out (insufficient CPU, tainted node, wrong zone affinity, volume not available). Green nodes remain.

Key fact: Resource requests drive scheduling — the scheduler looks at requests (not limits) to find nodes with enough remaining allocatable capacity.

title: Step 2 — Priorities score remaining nodes
content:
Lab step 2 — Pod Scheduling: Priorities score remaining nodes

Narration: After filtering, the scheduler scores remaining nodes using priorities: spreading pods across zones, packing to minimise nodes, preferring specific hardware. Highest score wins.

Visual: Three remaining nodes shown with score bars: Node A: 85/100, Node B: 72/100, Node C: 55/100. Arrow shows pod being placed on Node A. Scores labelled with contributing factors: 'spread: +40, resource balance: +45'.

Key fact: Predicates are pass/fail. Priorities determine the winner among eligible nodes. Affinity rules can be either a predicate (required) or a priority (preferred).

title: Step 1 — Rolling update with Deployment
content:
Lab step 1 — Workloads: Rolling update with Deployment

Narration: When you update a Deployment, Kubernetes creates a new ReplicaSet and gradually shifts pods from the old RS to the new one — zero downtime.

Visual: Timeline showing: RS-v1 (3 pods) → RS-v2 starts (1 pod), RS-v1 (2 pods) → RS-v2 (2), RS-v1 (1) → RS-v2 (3), RS-v1 (0). kubectl rollout undo arrow pointing back.

Key fact: kubectl rollout undo deployment webapp immediately reverses the update by scaling up the old ReplicaSet. The old RS is kept for exactly this purpose.

title: Step 2 — StatefulSet stable identity
content:
Lab step 2 — Workloads: StatefulSet stable identity

Narration: Unlike Deployments, StatefulSet pods get persistent names (mysql-0, mysql-1, mysql-2), a stable DNS hostname via a Headless Service, and each gets its own PersistentVolumeClaim.

Visual: Three pods with stable labels mysql-0, mysql-1, mysql-2. Each connected to its own EBS volume (PVC: data-mysql-0, etc.). Deployment pods shown alongside with random hash names for contrast.

Key fact: StatefulSets start in order (0 first) and terminate in reverse (2 first). Never scale a StatefulSet by random pod deletion — use kubectl scale.

title: Summary: Container Benefits
content:
Summary — Container Benefits

• **Portable**: Containers run on any OS with a container runtime — same image in dev, staging, prod, eliminating environment drift.
• **Declarative**: You describe desired state (3 replicas of nginx); Kubernetes reconciles the actual state to match — the foundation of self-healing.
• **Scalable**: Containers start in seconds — scale out by adding copies, scale in by removing them, with no architectural change required.

⚠ Exam trap: 'Continuous deployment' is about the SAME image running everywhere, not about speed of deployment pipelines.

title: Summary: Pods and Services
content:
Summary — Pods and Services

• **Pod**: Smallest deployable unit. Containers inside share network namespace (localhost) and optional volumes. Every pod gets a unique IP.
• **Service**: Stable ClusterIP + DNS name that load-balances to pods matching its label selector. Survives pod restarts.
• **Labels**: The glue between pods and services — selector: {app: backend} routes to all pods with that label.

⚠ Exam trap: A Pod is NOT a container. A Pod wraps containers. The exam will test whether you can distinguish between the two.

title: Summary: Kubernetes Internals
content:
Summary — Kubernetes Internals

• **etcd**: The cluster brain — stores ALL state as key-value pairs. Loss of etcd = loss of cluster. On EKS, AWS manages etcd.
• **kube-scheduler**: Assigns pods to nodes using predicates (filter) then priorities (score). Reads resource requests, not limits.
• **Control loop**: Controllers constantly compare desired state (manifests) to actual state (running) and reconcile any difference — this is Kubernetes self-healing.

⚠ Exam trap: On EKS, you cannot SSH into control plane nodes. AWS manages kube-apiserver, etcd, scheduler, and controller-manager entirely.

title: Summary: Pod Scheduling
content:
Summary — Pod Scheduling

• **Predicates**: Hard pass/fail filters — resource fit, taint toleration, node affinity, volume topology. All must pass.
• **Priorities**: Soft scoring of surviving nodes — spread, pack, zone preference. Highest score wins placement.
• **Resource requests**: What the scheduler reads to find eligible nodes. Setting requests too low = poor placement. Too high = pending pods.

⚠ Exam trap: The scheduler uses resource REQUESTS (guaranteed), not resource LIMITS (maximum), to determine if a node can host a pod.

title: Summary: kubectl and Objects
content:
Summary — kubectl and Objects

• **kubectl apply**: Declarative, idempotent. Creates if absent, patches if present. Always prefer over kubectl create in automation.
• **ConfigMap vs Secret**: ConfigMap = non-sensitive config. Secret = base64-encoded sensitive data (NOT encrypted by default). Both mountable as env vars or volumes.
• **DaemonSet**: One pod per node. Used for node-level agents (Fluent Bit, aws-node). NOT supported on Fargate.

⚠ Exam trap: Kubernetes Secrets are base64 encoded, not encrypted. Anyone with kubectl get secret access can decode them instantly.

title: Summary: Workloads
content:
Summary — Workloads

• **Deployment**: For stateless apps. Rolling updates via ReplicaSet replacement. kubectl rollout undo for instant rollback.
• **StatefulSet**: For stateful apps. Stable pod names (mysql-0,1,2), own PVC per pod, ordered startup/shutdown.
• **Job/CronJob**: Run-to-completion workloads. Job runs once; CronJob schedules Jobs periodically.

⚠ Exam trap: DaemonSets are NOT supported on AWS Fargate. If you need a node-level agent (like Fluent Bit), you must use EC2 node groups.
